pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head without acute intracranial process, subtle cerebral edema
pertinent_positives.special: COHb 28% confirming carbon monoxide poisoning — normal SpO2 was misleading

report_template.assessment: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning — occupational, chronic subacute exposure with acute-on-chronic episode, complicated by myocardial injury and encephalopathy
report_template.plan: 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask, admit for cardiac monitoring, hyperbaric oxygen therapy (COHb >25% with neurological symptoms), serial troponins, neurology follow-up for delayed sequelae, mandatory reporting and evacuation of furnace hall

explanation: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with 250x the affinity of oxygen, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) and preventing oxygen delivery. Key: SpO2 is falsely normal — it cannot distinguish COHb from OHb. Classic presentation: headache/nausea/confusion worse at work, better on days off, multiple people affected. Treatment: 100% O2 via NRB (half-life 60-90 min vs 5hr on room air). HBO if COHb >25%, neuro symptoms, cardiac involvement, pregnancy.
fennick_opening: "He apologized before he sat down. That is the first thing you should write."
cc_after_addl_hx: I should have come in sooner. I'm sorry if I should have come in sooner. The other men have the headaches too but they haven't come in.
cc_after_pe: Walk in a straight line. With my eyes closed. I — sorry. Let me try again.
cc_after_imaging: The brain looks normal. Then why does my head feel like this.
cc_after_hint: The other men. Two of them said the same thing last week. I thought it was just the long shifts.
cc_after_dx: The furnace. It's been the furnace the whole time. I sealed those ducts myself. Last November.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Worse at work. Better on days off. Two colleagues. Ventilation sealed for winter. The hall is still running right now."
fennick_after_pe: "SpO2 97% and encephalopathic. The numbers do not match the patient in front of you. Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin. That reading is a lie."
fennick_after_imaging: "CT normal. Brain is not the source — it is the target. Order the test the standard panel does not include."

title: The Locksmith's Sudden Chest

chart.cc: I was just sitting at my workbench this morning and something... popped. In my chest. Now it hurts to breathe.
chart.hpi: The Locksmith arrived on foot, slightly hunched and breathing shallowly. He was at his workbench in the Meridian Key Works when he felt a sudden sharp pain in his left chest — he describes it precisely as a pop, followed immediately by shortness of breath. There was no exertion, no trauma, no coughing. He is tall and lean, and this is actually his second such episode — the first occurred eighteen months ago, resolved on its own, and he never sought attention for it.
chart.fh: No family history of lung disease. No known connective tissue disorders.
chart.sh: Apprentice locksmith, works seated at a precision workbench. Lives with his mother. Non-smoker. Occasional drink at the guild hall.

additional_hx: Answers directly, no embellishment. First episode 18 months ago — same side, same pop, resolved after two days rest. Assumed pulled muscle. This time has not improved and breathlessness is more noticeable. Tall, over six feet, lean. Never smoked. No illness, no cough, no fever. Asks matter-of-factly whether he will need an operation.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "It happened once before, about a year and a half ago. Same side, same feeling. I just rested and it went away after a couple of days. I figured it was a pulled muscle. Should I have come in for that one?"
pe_findings: Tall 188cm, BMI 19.2. Breathing shallowly to minimise pain. Decreased breath sounds left base. Hyperresonance to percussion left hemithorax. Trachea midline. No subcutaneous emphysema. Tachycardic, regular.
imaging: CXR PA: Left-sided pneumothorax — visible pleural line with absent lung markings peripheral to it. Estimated 30% left lung collapse. No mediastinal shift. No effusion. Right lung clear. No underlying parenchymal disease or blebs visible on plain film. CT chest recommended to assess for subpleural blebs.

special_test.name: CT Chest (High Resolution)
special_test.result: Multiple bilateral subpleural blebs — largest 1.4cm at left upper lobe apex. Left pneumothorax confirmed at 30% collapse. No underlying parenchymal disease. No evidence of Marfan syndrome changes on vascular imaging. Findings consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax with bilateral bleb disease — significantly elevated recurrence risk. Surgical referral (VATS pleurodesis) strongly recommended given second episode.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. WBC 9.2, Hgb 14.8, Plt 334. No infection or inflammatory process.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Completely normal. No metabolic derangement.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 94. Low voltage QRS left-sided leads — consistent with left pneumothorax. No ischemic changes. No right heart strain pattern. Not a PE pattern.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No myocardial injury. This is not a cardiac event.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant to this presentation.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.44. pO2 78 (mildly reduced on room air — consistent with 30% lung collapse and V/Q mismatch). pCO2 34. Mild hypoxemia but compensated. Not immediately life-threatening.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. Relevant if chest tube or surgical intervention planned.

hints: Tall, lean, young man. Sudden sharp chest pain with a pop — no trauma, no exertion, no warning. This has happened before. What diagnosis recurs in tall thin young men?
Decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance on the left. Trachea midline. This is not tension — there is no mediastinal shift. But 30% collapse is not trivial.
This is his second spontaneous pneumothorax on the same side. The first he ignored. Current guidelines recommend intervention after a second ipsilateral episode — recurrence risk is now over 80%.

Primary spontaneous pneumothorax: no underlying lung disease, young healthy patient, subpleural blebs. Secondary spontaneous: underlying COPD, CF, Marfan. Always rule out secondary causes — check for connective tissue features.

management.title: Immediate management of 30% pneumothorax
management.hint: 30% left pneumothorax, O2 95%, stable, no tension signs. What is the appropriate intervention?

options.text: Observation only — it will reabsorb on its own
options.feedback_wrong: Observation alone is appropriate only for small (<20%) asymptomatic pneumothoraces. This patient is symptomatic with O2 95% and a 30% collapse. Active intervention is indicated.

Thoracotomy is not indicated for a stable primary spontaneous pneumothorax. There is no tension, no hemothorax, no mediastinal shift. Needle aspiration or chest tube are the appropriate interventions.

options.text: Supplemental O2 and needle aspiration or small-bore chest tube
options.feedback_correct: Correct. 30% pneumothorax in a symptomatic patient warrants intervention. Needle aspiration first-line for primary spontaneous PTX. High-flow O2 accelerates nitrogen resorption.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Aspiration first. If the lung re-expands and stays up, you may avoid a chest tube. If it does not, or recurs, tube comes next."

options.feedback_wrong: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed pneumothorax risks converting it to a tension pneumothorax. Never ventilate a patient with a known pneumothorax before decompression. This would be dangerous.

management.title: Second episode — definitive management
management.hint: This is his second left-sided spontaneous pneumothorax. What does current guidance recommend?

options.feedback_correct: Correct. Second ipsilateral episode plus bilateral bleb disease on CT — VATS with bullectomy and pleurodesis. Recurrence rate after second episode exceeds 80%.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "VATS allows direct resection of the blebs, not just pleurodesis through a tube. In a young patient with bilateral disease, mechanical pleurodesis at the time of bullectomy gives the best long-term outcome."

options.feedback_wrong: This is primary spontaneous pneumothorax — there is no underlying airway disease to treat. Bronchodilators have no role here. This is a structural problem requiring surgical correction.

options.text: Discharge and advise to return if it recurs again
options.feedback_wrong: Waiting for a third episode is not guideline-concordant and puts the patient at serious risk. A third spontaneous pneumothorax can be life-threatening. The second episode is the standard threshold for surgical referral.

options.text: Chemical pleurodesis via chest tube only
options.feedback_wrong: Chemical pleurodesis via chest tube is less effective than VATS in young patients with bleb disease. VATS allows direct visualization and resection of blebs as well as mechanical and chemical pleurodesis — superior outcomes and lower recurrence rates.

management.title: Rule out secondary cause
management.hint: Tall, lean, 22 years old. Second spontaneous pneumothorax. What must you actively exclude?

options.text: Bronchoscopy to rule out endobronchial lesion
options.feedback_wrong: Bronchoscopy is not indicated for primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a young healthy patient. There is no clinical suspicion for an endobronchial lesion causing this presentation.

HIV-related pneumothorax is typically secondary to PCP pneumonia and presents differently — with bilateral infiltrates, hypoxia, and systemic illness. This young non-immunocompromised patient has primary spontaneous PTX with blebs. HIV testing is not the priority here.

options.text: Assess for Marfan syndrome — echocardiogram and genetics referral
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Marfan syndrome associated with spontaneous PTX. Formal assessment: arm span, segment ratio, echocardiogram for aortic root. The aortic root is the reason Marfan matters beyond the pneumothorax.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Aortic root dilation, progressive, dissection risk. You are not just screening for a diagnosis — you are looking for a potentially fatal complication he does not yet know he has."

options.text: No workup needed — primary spontaneous PTX requires no further investigation
options.feedback_wrong: A second episode in a young patient always warrants investigation for secondary causes, particularly connective tissue disorders. Marfan syndrome specifically needs to be excluded — it carries serious aortic implications beyond the pneumothorax.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: Treated, stable, O2 improving. Where does he go after chest tube or aspiration?

options.text: Discharge with advice to avoid exercise and flying
options.feedback_wrong: Activity restrictions alone are not definitive management for a second ipsilateral spontaneous pneumothorax with known bleb disease. This patient needs surgical referral and admission — not a list of things to avoid.

options.text: Discharge immediately after aspiration if CXR improved
options.feedback_wrong: Same-day discharge after aspiration is reasonable only for small first-episode pneumothoraces in reliable patients close to hospital. This is a second episode with bilateral blebs — he needs admission and surgical planning.

options.text: Transfer to thoracic surgery for immediate VATS today
options.feedback_wrong: VATS is not performed acutely on a fresh pneumothorax. The lung needs time to re-expand fully before surgery. Admit, decompress, re-expand, then plan elective VATS.

options.text: Admit for observation, repeat CXR, thoracic surgery consult
options.feedback_correct: Correct. After intervention, admit for 24-hour observation with repeat CXR to confirm re-expansion. Thoracic surgery consult for VATS planning given second episode. Marfan workup. Do not discharge until lung is fully re-expanded and stable.

report_template.pmh: prior spontaneous left pneumothorax 18 months ago, not formally treated
report_template.presenting: sudden left-sided chest pain and dyspnea at rest — second episode of spontaneous pneumothorax

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: second ipsilateral episode, first ignored 18 months ago, tall lean habitus BMI 19, no trauma or exertion precipitant
pertinent_positives.pe: decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance left hemithorax, trachea midline, O2 95%
pertinent_positives.labs: mild hypoxemia pO2 78 on ABG, low voltage on EKG left leads consistent with PTX
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR showing 30% left pneumothorax with visible pleural line
pertinent_positives.special: CT chest confirming multiple bilateral subpleural blebs, largest 1.4cm left upper lobe — surgical bleb disease confirmed

report_template.assessment: Primary Spontaneous Pneumothorax — second episode, bilateral bleb disease on CT, surgical intervention indicated
report_template.plan: needle aspiration or small-bore chest tube, supplemental O2, admit for observation and repeat CXR, thoracic surgery consult for VATS pleurodesis, Marfan syndrome assessment

explanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP) occurs in tall, thin young men without underlying lung disease — subpleural blebs rupture at the apex. Recurrence after first episode ~30%, after second >80%. Treat first episode: aspiration or small-bore chest tube. Second ipsilateral episode: VATS bullectomy + pleurodesis. Always rule out Marfan syndrome in young patients with recurrent PSP.
fennick_opening: "Second time. Same side. He ignored the first."
cc_after_addl_hx: The first time I thought it was a pulled muscle. Should I have come in for that one.
cc_after_pe: You're tapping on my chest. What does the difference in sound tell you.
cc_after_imaging: Thirty percent of the left lung. That seems like something that should have been addressed the first time.
cc_after_hint: Second time. Same side. I didn't think resting it the first time was the wrong call.
cc_after_dx: It has a name. And I need an operation. I can see that from your face.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Tall, lean, young, second ipsilateral episode. The first was 18 months ago and he ignored it. Recurrence rate after a second episode exceeds 80%."
fennick_after_pe: "Hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, trachea midline. Not tension. But 30% collapse in a symptomatic patient is not observation territory."
fennick_after_imaging: "Bilateral blebs. Largest 1.4cm, left upper lobe. Second episode, bilateral disease. The surgical threshold has been met."

title: The Herbalist's Affliction

chart.cc: Something is very wrong. My mouth has gone completely dry, I can't see properly, and I feel like my heart is going to come through my chest. I've been working with belladonna all morning.
chart.hpi: The Herbalist arrived flushed and frightened, still holding dried belladonna stems, her pupils so dilated she walked into the doorframe. She has been preparing a belladonna tincture in her apothecary workshop in the Ashgate botanical quarter since early morning — grinding the berries and leaves without gloves. Over the past two hours she has developed profound dry mouth, blurred vision, palpitations, and confusion that is worsening. She also reports that she cannot urinate despite feeling she needs to. Her skin is hot and dry. She has not ingested the plant deliberately but significant dermal and possible mucosal absorption has occurred.
chart.pmh: No significant medical history. Works with botanical compounds daily.
chart.meds: None prescribed. Uses various herbal preparations — none containing atropine or anticholinergics.
chart.sh: Botanical herbalist and apothecary, Ashgate quarter. Lives above her workshop. Non-smoker. Does not drink.

additional_hx: She volunteers the exposure history before you ask — knows exactly what happened. Large fresh belladonna harvest, bare-handed grinding for two hours, rubbed her face. Vision went first, then dry mouth, then heart racing. Could not swallow water properly. Has not urinated in four hours. Warm all over, completely dry despite obvious distress.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "I know what this is. I know it. I just — I didn't think it would happen through the skin. I wasn't even eating it. I've handled it before but never for this long. How bad is it? Please just tell me how bad it is."
pe_findings: Flushed, hot, dry skin — no diaphoresis. Agitated, frightened, oriented. Pupils fixed and maximally dilated 8mm bilaterally. Photophobia. Profoundly dry mucous membranes, thick ropy saliva. HR 128, BP 148/92. Distended bladder on percussion, tender. Bowel sounds absent.
imaging: CXR: Normal. No aspiration pneumonia. CT Head: No acute intracranial process. Performed given altered mentation — normal. Bladder ultrasound: 680mL retained urine — acute urinary retention confirmed.

special_test.name: Serum and Urine Toxicology Screen
special_test.result: Serum: atropine/scopolamine detected — consistent with Atropa belladonna alkaloid absorption. No other toxins detected. No acetaminophen, no salicylates, no ethanol, no opiates. Urine toxicology: positive for tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine). Confirms anticholinergic toxidrome from belladonna alkaloid absorption. Quantitative levels not immediately available but clinical syndrome is diagnostic.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: WBC 13.2 (mildly elevated — stress response/atropine effect). Hgb 13.8. Plt 310. No infection pattern.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Glucose 142 (mildly elevated — anticholinergic effect). Cr 1.1. BUN 18. Na 141, K 3.6. LFTs normal. No metabolic acidosis. Electrolytes normal.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 128. Prolonged QT interval 480ms — anticholinergic cardiotoxicity. No ventricular arrhythmia currently. QT must be monitored — risk of Torsades de Pointes. No STEMI pattern.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No primary myocardial injury. Tachycardia is from anticholinergic effect, not myocardial damage.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Absent bowel sounds are from anticholinergic ileus, not pancreatitis.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Concentrated urine — markedly decreased output. No infection. Urinary retention confirmed clinically and on bladder scan. Straight catheterization required.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.43. pO2 96. pCO2 38. Normal. No respiratory compromise currently. Monitor for hyperthermia-induced deterioration.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. No coagulopathy.

hints: Dry as a bone, blind as a bat, red as a beet, hot as a hare, mad as a hatter. She has been handling belladonna for two hours without gloves. These five findings together have a name.

Her pupils are fixed and maximally dilated. Her skin is hot and dry — no sweat at all. Her heart is racing. Her bowels have stopped. She cannot urinate. Every secretion in her body has been blocked. What receptor does atropine block?

Anticholinergic toxidrome — muscarinic blockade. The antidote is physostigmine, a reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor. It crosses the blood-brain barrier. Use with caution — can cause seizures and bradycardia if given too fast.

Do not confuse with sympathomimetic toxidrome — that also has tachycardia, hyperthermia, and agitation BUT has diaphoresis (wet skin). Anticholinergic is DRY. Sympathomimetic is WET. That distinction is the diagnosis.

management.title: Immediate supportive management
management.hint: HR 128, temp 38.6, agitated, urinary retention. What are the first priorities?

options.feedback_wrong: Activated charcoal is not appropriate here — absorption has already occurred dermally and the exposure was hours ago. Furthermore, anticholinergic ileus means gut motility is absent, increasing aspiration risk. Contraindicated in this context.

options.text: IV access, benzodiazepines for agitation, urinary catheter, cooling measures
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Benzodiazepines for agitation — not haloperidol, which worsens QT. Catheter for 680mL retention. Active cooling — anhidrosis means she cannot thermoregulate.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "She cannot sweat. Without diaphoresis, hyperthermia will continue to climb. Cooling is not comfort — it is treatment."

options.feedback_wrong: She did not ingest the plant — this was dermal exposure. Inducing emesis does nothing. Additionally, anticholinergics cause ileus and dry mucous membranes making emesis dangerous and ineffective.

Haloperidol is contraindicated in anticholinergic toxidrome — it has additional anticholinergic activity and prolongs the QT interval further. This patient already has QT 480ms. Benzodiazepines are the correct sedative agent here.
options.feedback_correct: Haloperidol has anticholinergic activity and prolongs QT. She already has QT 480ms. This worsens the toxidrome and increases arrhythmia risk simultaneously.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "QT 480 already. You gave her a drug with anticholinergic side effects and QT prolongation. The benzos work centrally without making any of this worse."

management.title: Specific antidote
management.hint: Classic anticholinergic toxidrome. Is there a specific antidote and when do you use it?

options.feedback_wrong: Naloxone reverses opioid toxidrome — sedation, miosis, respiratory depression. This patient has anticholinergic toxidrome — mydriasis, tachycardia, hyperthermia, dry skin. Completely opposite picture. Naloxone will do nothing.

options.text: Physostigmine IV — reversible AChE inhibitor, crosses BBB
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Physostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase, raising ACh to overcome muscarinic blockade. Crosses BBB — effective centrally. Slow IV push — bradycardia and seizures if pushed too fast.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The dose matters. Push it fast and you create cholinergic crisis. Slow push, atropine at bedside. It is an antidote, not an emergency bolus."

options.feedback_wrong: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines. She has not taken benzodiazepines — she has belladonna alkaloid poisoning. Flumazenil has no role here and would remove the benzodiazepines you just gave her for agitation control.

Atropine IS the poison here — giving more atropine would catastrophically worsen the toxidrome. This is one of the most dangerous errors in toxicology. Never give atropine for anticholinergic toxidrome.
options.feedback_correct: Atropine is the toxin. Giving more atropine to an anticholinergic toxidrome is catastrophic.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "You gave the poison as the antidote."

management.title: Monitor for this serious complication
management.hint: QT 480ms, tachycardia 128, hyperthermia. What are you watching for on the monitor?

options.feedback_wrong: Anticholinergic toxidrome can cause fatal arrhythmias, hyperthermia-induced seizures, and rhabdomyolysis. It requires active monitoring and management. 'Self-limiting' is only true with proper supportive care — it is not benign.

options.text: Torsades de Pointes — QT prolongation monitoring and magnesium ready
options.feedback_correct: Correct. QT 480ms with tachycardia creates significant risk of Torsades de Pointes — a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that can degenerate into VF. Continuous cardiac monitoring is mandatory. IV magnesium sulfate should be available. Avoid any additional QT-prolonging agents.

options.feedback_wrong: Hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis is not the primary concern in anticholinergic toxidrome. The immediate threat is QT prolongation and Torsades de Pointes from anticholinergic cardiotoxicity.

Pulmonary edema is not the primary concern here. The main arrhythmia risk from anticholinergic QT prolongation is Torsades de Pointes. This patient needs cardiac monitoring, not respiratory precautions specifically.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: HR 128, QT 480ms, temp 38.6, urinary retention, agitation. Where does she go?

options.feedback_wrong: Physostigmine is an IV medication used in acute toxidrome management — it is not dispensed as an outpatient oral prescription. This patient requires inpatient monitoring and IV management.

options.text: Discharge after 2 hours of observation if vitals improve
options.feedback_wrong: Two hours is not adequate. Belladonna alkaloids have a prolonged duration of action — effects can last 24-48 hours. QT prolongation and hyperthermia require sustained monitoring. She needs admission.

options.text: Transfer to psychiatry for agitation management
options.feedback_wrong: Her agitation is organic — caused by anticholinergic delirium. This is a medical emergency, not a psychiatric presentation. Psychiatric transfer would be inappropriate and dangerous without medical stabilization.

options.text: Admit to monitored bed — toxicology, continuous telemetry, physostigmine if needed
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Admission with continuous telemetry is mandatory given QT prolongation and tachycardia. Toxicology consult. Serial vital signs and neuro assessments. She will likely improve over 12-24 hours as the alkaloids are metabolized, but the arrhythmia window requires monitoring.

report_template.presenting: acute anticholinergic toxidrome following two hours of unprotected dermal exposure to Atropa belladonna during herbalist work

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: two hours bare-handed grinding of belladonna berries and leaves, facial contact, symptom onset within two hours — blurred vision first, then dry mouth, then palpitations, now urinary retention
pertinent_positives.pe: fixed maximally dilated pupils 8mm bilaterally, flushed hot dry skin with complete anhidrosis, HR 128, temp 38.6, distended bladder with 680mL retention on ultrasound, absent bowel sounds
pertinent_positives.labs: mildly elevated glucose, QT prolongation to 480ms on EKG — arrhythmia risk
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head without acute intracranial process, bladder scan confirming urinary retention
pertinent_positives.special: serum and urine toxicology positive for atropine and tropane alkaloids confirming belladonna anticholinergic toxidrome

report_template.assessment: Anticholinergic Toxidrome — Atropa belladonna dermal and mucosal exposure, complicated by urinary retention, ileus, hyperthermia, and QT prolongation
report_template.plan: IV benzodiazepines for agitation, urinary catheterization, active cooling, continuous telemetry for QT monitoring, IV magnesium on standby, physostigmine if refractory agitation or hemodynamic compromise, toxicology consult, admit for 24-48hr monitoring

explanation: Anticholinergic toxidrome: dry as a bone (anhidrosis), blind as a bat (mydriasis), red as a beet (flushing), hot as a hare (hyperthermia), mad as a hatter (delirium). Caused by muscarinic blockade — atropine, scopolamine, belladonna, jimsonweed, diphenhydramine. Antidote: physostigmine (crosses BBB). Never give atropine. Never give haloperidol. Distinguish from sympathomimetic (also hot/tachy/agitated but DIAPHORETIC — key difference).
fennick_opening: "She knows exactly what happened. She told you before you asked."
cc_after_addl_hx: I know what this is. I know it. I just didn't think it would happen through the skin. I've handled it before. Never for this long.
cc_after_pe: 8 millimetres. I could feel them not responding. Like they belonged to someone else.
cc_after_imaging: 680 millilitres. I can feel every millilitre of it. I could not understand why I couldn't go.
cc_after_hint: How bad is it. Please just tell me how bad it is.
cc_after_dx: The alkaloids. Through the skin. I know the pharmacology. I just couldn't believe I'd managed to dose myself through my hands.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Two hours of direct contact with fresh belladonna. She gave you the exposure history before you asked. The rest is clinical confirmation."
fennick_after_pe: "Dry, hot, flushed, mydriasis, tachycardia, retention, silent bowel. Every secretion blocked. Muscarinic antagonism made visible."
fennick_after_imaging: "Bladder full, bowel silent, head clear. The CNS is the remaining concern. You have an antidote. Use it before the delirium worsens."

title: The Seamstress's Sorrow

chart.cc: I don't know why I'm here, really. I'm just tired. All the time. And sad, I suppose. My supervisor sent me. She says I've been crying at my bench.
chart.hpi: The Seamstress arrived alone, her coat buttoned to the collar despite the mild evening. She moves carefully, as if conserving something. She has been the lead embroiderer at the Threadneedle Textile Works for six years — fine detail work under lamplight, twelve-hour shifts. Over the past four months she has felt a creeping, total exhaustion that she cannot sleep off. She weeps without knowing why. She has lost interest in her embroidery patterns, which once consumed her. Her supervisor noticed she had been making errors — dropped stitches, misaligned seams — and sent her here with a note that reads simply: 'Something is wrong with her.' She was treated for depression at a guild physician two months ago. The sertraline has done nothing. Her menstrual cycles have always been heavy — she mentions this only when directly asked, as if it is too ordinary a detail to bother with.
chart.pmh: Depression — diagnosed two months ago at guild physician. Dysmenorrhea.
chart.meds: Sertraline 50mg daily (started 8 weeks ago). Iron-poor diet — skips meals during busy production seasons.
chart.fh: Mother: anaemia in her thirties, details unknown. Father: well.
chart.sh: Master seamstress, Threadneedle Textile Works, Cogsworth District. Lives alone in the workers' quarter above the mill. Twelve-hour shifts, six days a week. Non-smoker. Does not drink. Has not been eating properly — bread and tea most days during the winter rush.

cc_after_addl_hx: The periods have always been heavy. I just thought — I thought that was normal. Nobody ever told me it wasn't.
cc_after_pe: My nails. I've been filing them down for months. I didn't know they were trying to tell me something.
cc_after_imaging: My heart is alright though? At least tell me my heart is alright.
cc_after_hint: Please. I just want to know what's wrong with me. I'm so tired of not knowing.
cc_after_dx: Iron. All these months. And it was just iron.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Seven to eight days. Clots. And not one person in her life thought to ask whether that was normal. Not one."
fennick_after_pe: "The colour of old linen. That is not depression. That is a woman running dry."
fennick_after_imaging: "Heart is holding. The flow murmur is compensatory — high output state from anaemia. The structure is fine. Look at the blood."
additional_hx: She answers with quiet precision, as if following instructions. Periods: always heavy, seven to eight days, soaking, clots. Never sought help — no one told her she should. Craves ice constantly from the cart outside the mill. Embarrassed to mention it. Not eating real meals since November. Short of breath on the stairs every morning — thought she was just unfit. The sertraline has not touched the sadness. She looks at her hands when she says this.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "I keep thinking I should be able to push through it. I've always pushed through things. But it's like pushing through water. And the crying — I don't even know what I'm crying about. I just cry. The ice helps somehow. I know that sounds strange."
pe_findings: Pale, thin, fatigued. Koilonychia — bilateral spoon-shaped nails, worst at index and middle fingers. Angular cheilitis. Conjunctival pallor bilaterally — palpebral conjunctivae colour of old linen. Mild glossitis. HR 108, regular. Soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal border.
imaging: CXR: Normal cardiac silhouette. No infiltrate, no effusion. Mild cardiomegaly not present — the flow murmur is functional. No bony abnormalities. No hilar lymphadenopathy. Not the diagnostic study of choice here — blood work is what you need.

special_test.name: Peripheral Blood Smear
special_test.result: Hypochromic microcytic red blood cells — pale centres larger than normal, pencil cells, occasional target cells. Marked anisocytosis and poikilocytosis. RDW elevated. Platelets mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis — classic in iron deficiency). No spherocytes, no schistocytes, no hypersegmented neutrophils. Morphology is unambiguously consistent with iron deficiency anaemia.

memory_fragments.cbc: The roses she embroidered once blessed strangers' weddings with red. She cannot remember now what red feels like. As if the red inside her has simply... run dry.
memory_fragments.pe_koilonychia: Her nails built a thousand things. Now they are spoons — hollow, curved inward, as if her own hands are begging to be filled with something she no longer has.
memory_fragments.addl_hx_ice: Chewing ice is the only moment of clarity she gets. In the heat of the mill, surrounded by steam and noise, it is the last cold thing keeping her from dissolving entirely.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Hgb 7.4 g/dL (CRITICALLY LOW). MCV 64 fL (LOW — microcytic). MCH 19 pg (LOW). MCHC 28 g/dL (LOW — hypochromic). RDW 18.2% (HIGH). WBC 7.1 (normal). Platelets 487 (mildly elevated — reactive thrombocytosis, classic in iron deficiency).
tests.memory_fragment: The roses she embroidered once blessed strangers' weddings with red. She cannot remember now what red feels like. As if the red inside her has simply... run dry.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 0.7, glucose 82, LFTs normal, albumin 3.4 (mildly low — poor nutritional intake). No metabolic derangement.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 108. No ischemic changes. Tachycardia is a compensatory response to severe anaemia — high-output state. No arrhythmia.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No myocardial injury. The flow murmur is not from structural cardiac disease.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant to this presentation.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No haematuria. No protein. No infection. No haemoglobinuria to suggest haemolysis.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.42, pO2 96 on room air. Normal. Her oxygen saturation is fine — the problem is not oxygenation, it is oxygen-carrying capacity. SpO2 is misleading in severe anaemia.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. PT/INR 1.0, PTT 28. No coagulopathy. Heavy menstrual bleeding is not from a clotting disorder in this case.

hints: The sertraline has done nothing in eight weeks. That is not a treatment failure — that is a diagnostic clue. Her sadness is not primary. It is downstream of something physical. What does profound anaemia do to a person's mood, energy, and cognition?
Tachycardia at rest, flow murmur, conjunctival pallor, koilonychia, angular cheilitis, glossitis, craving ice. She is not depressed. She is exsanguinating slowly. The CBC will tell you everything.
Microcytic hypochromic anaemia in a menstruating woman who skips meals and has heavy periods for seven days every cycle. The iron stores are empty. Check ferritin, serum iron, TIBC.
Pica — the craving for ice specifically — is called pagophagia. It is one of the most specific signs of iron deficiency anaemia. When a patient tells you they cannot stop eating ice, believe them.

management.title: Immediate investigation to confirm
management.hint: The CBC strongly suggests iron deficiency. What do you order to confirm it biochemically before starting treatment?

options.feedback_correct: Confirmed. Ferritin depletes before Hgb falls — most sensitive marker. In iron deficiency: ferritin low, serum iron low, TIBC high.
options.pt_remark_correct: So the numbers will prove it. That something is actually wrong. That I wasn't just weak.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Ferritin is an acute phase reactant — it can be falsely normal in inflammation. At Hgb 7.4 with this history, you will not find a false normal. Check it."

options.feedback_wrong: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue and depression — but it causes macrocytic or normocytic anaemia, not microcytic. The CBC is already showing you the picture. Follow it.
options.pt_remark_wrong: My thyroid. Could it be my thyroid? I hadn't thought of that.

options.feedback_wrong: She is not depressed. Checking whether she is taking her sertraline misses the point entirely. The CBC is telling you what is wrong.
options.pt_remark_wrong: You want to check if I've been taking the pills. I have. Every morning. They just... don't work.

options.feedback_wrong: There is nothing in this presentation to suggest intracranial pathology. The CBC has already handed you the diagnosis. An MRI brain tells you nothing useful here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: There's something in my brain? I — I don't think it's my brain.

management.title: First-line treatment
management.hint: Iron deficiency confirmed. Hgb 7.4. She is symptomatic but haemodynamically stable. What do you start?

options.text: Oral ferrous sulfate 325mg TID with vitamin C
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Ferrous sulfate with vitamin C. Hgb 7.4, haemodynamically stable — oral iron is appropriate. Transfusion threshold in stable patients is below 7.0.
fennick_speech: "Stop. Before you write that prescription — look at her."
"The dry riverbed of her veins is about to receive rain for the first time in months. The iron will flow back. The red will return — slowly, not all at once, but it will return."

"In a week her reticulocytes will surge. In four weeks her Hgb will climb. And somewhere around week six... she will reach for a needle without thinking about it. Not because she decided to. Because her hands will remember before she does."
"We did not treat depression today. We returned the thread to the woman who makes beautiful things."
options.pt_remark_correct: Iron. Red. I used to know what that felt like.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Vitamin C reduces ferric to ferrous iron in the gut — meaningfully increases absorption. It is not optional."

options.feedback_wrong: IV iron is for patients who cannot tolerate oral iron or have malabsorption. Hgb 7.4 with no haemodynamic compromise — oral iron is appropriate. Do not over-escalate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can take anything. A drip, pills, whatever. I just want to feel like myself again.

options.feedback_wrong: Transfusion threshold in stable non-bleeding patients is Hgb below 7.0 — and even then, only with cardiovascular compromise. She is tachycardic but maintaining. Oral iron is correct.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A transfusion sounds frightening. Is it that bad?

options.feedback_wrong: There is no depression to treat. The sadness, the anhedonia, the fog — all manifestations of severe anaemia. Increasing her sertraline will do precisely nothing.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More sertraline. I knew you'd say more sertraline.
options.feedback_correct: There is no depression to treat. Sadness, anhedonia, cognitive fog — all downstream of Hgb 7.4. Iron will lift the mood within weeks.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Eight weeks of sertraline already tried. Already failed. What does a higher dose accomplish."
options.pt_remark_correct: More sertraline. I knew you would say more sertraline.

management.title: Address the underlying cause
management.hint: Treating the anaemia without addressing why it developed is incomplete medicine. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Refer to gynaecology for heavy menstrual bleeding
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Heavy menstrual bleeding is the most common cause of IDA in premenopausal women. She has been losing blood every cycle for years without anyone asking. Gynaecology is not optional.
options.pt_remark_correct: Someone is finally going to ask about it. In six years, no one has ever asked about it.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Oral iron without gynaecology referral means she is back here in six months with the same haemoglobin."

options.feedback_wrong: Diet is a contributing factor but dietary changes alone cannot compensate for seven days of heavy menstrual bleeding every month. Address the source.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I'll eat more. I will. I know I haven't been.

options.text: Upper GI endoscopy to rule out bleeding ulcer
options.feedback_wrong: GI investigation is appropriate in postmenopausal women or men where the source is not obvious. In a 29-year-old woman with heavy periods, the source is almost certainly menstrual. Do not scope her before addressing the obvious cause.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A scope. Inside. I — alright. If you think it's necessary.

options.text: No further workup — iron stores will replete with supplements
options.feedback_wrong: Treating the anaemia without finding why it keeps recurring means she will be back in six months with the same Hgb. The underlying cause is gynaecological. Patching a leak without finding the pipe.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Alright. Just the iron then.

management.title: Disposition and follow-up
management.hint: Stable patient, new diagnosis, outpatient-appropriate condition. What is your plan?

options.text: Discharge — ferrous sulfate, stop sertraline, gynaecology referral, recheck CBC in 4 weeks
options.feedback_correct: Exactly right. She goes home with iron, a clear explanation of what was actually wrong with her, instructions to stop the sertraline, and a referral to gynaecology. Recheck CBC and iron studies in 4 weeks. Tell her the ice cravings will go away once her iron is repleted. Tell her she is not broken.
options.pt_remark_correct: Four weeks. I can hold on for four weeks. And then — the needles again. The red thread.

options.text: Admit for IV iron and monitoring
options.feedback_wrong: There is no indication for admission. She is haemodynamically stable, not actively bleeding, and oral iron will work.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I don't need to stay here. I just need to go home.

options.text: Discharge with increased sertraline dose and iron
options.feedback_wrong: The depression is the anaemia. Once the iron is repleted, the mood will follow — reliably, within weeks. Continuing the sertraline perpetuates a misdiagnosis.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More sertraline. I — I'll take it. If you think it's right.

options.text: Refer to psychiatry for treatment-resistant depression
options.feedback_wrong: Referring her to psychiatry now would be the final insult of a long diagnostic failure. She does not have treatment-resistant depression. She has a Hgb of 7.4 and empty iron stores.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can see a psychiatrist. If that's what you think is wrong.

fennick_case_complete: "She came in here carrying a diagnosis someone handed her because they did not look hard enough. Depression. Eight weeks of sertraline for a woman whose body was simply running out of the thing that makes blood red."
"Every tear at her workbench. Every dropped stitch. Every morning she stood at the bottom of those stairs and wondered why her legs felt like iron — because they were starved of it."
"We did not cure her today. We found her. That is what diagnosis actually is — not naming a disease, but finding the person underneath it."
"She will pick up the red thread again. I promise you that."

report_template.pmh: previously misdiagnosed depression, dysmenorrhea, and poor nutritional intake
report_template.presenting: four months of progressive fatigue, low mood, and functional decline — referred by employer after errors at work

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: seven to eight day heavy menstrual cycles with clots, pagophagia (ice craving), dyspnea on exertion, no improvement on eight weeks of sertraline, skipping meals since November
pertinent_positives.pe: conjunctival pallor, koilonychia, angular cheilitis, glossitis, diffuse hair thinning, compensatory tachycardia rate 108 with soft systolic flow murmur
pertinent_positives.labs: Hgb 7.4 critically low, MCV 64 microcytic, RDW 18.2 elevated, reactive thrombocytosis — classic iron deficiency pattern
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR without acute findings, functional flow murmur confirmed not structural
pertinent_positives.special: peripheral smear showing hypochromic microcytic RBCs with pencil cells and target cells consistent with iron deficiency anaemia

report_template.assessment: Iron Deficiency Anaemia secondary to chronic heavy menstrual blood loss and nutritional deficiency, presenting as treatment-resistant depression
report_template.plan: discontinue sertraline, start ferrous sulfate 325mg TID with vitamin C, gynaecology referral for heavy menstrual bleeding workup, dietary counselling, recheck CBC and iron studies in 4 weeks

explanation: Iron deficiency anaemia is the most common anaemia worldwide. In premenopausal women, heavy menstrual bleeding is the leading cause. The classic presentation is microcytic hypochromic anaemia — low Hgb, low MCV, low ferritin, high TIBC. Peripheral smear: pencil cells, hypochromic microcytic RBCs. Clinical signs: koilonychia, angular cheilitis, glossitis, pagophagia (pica for ice). Fatigue and mood disturbance are common and completely reversible with iron repletion. Treatment: oral ferrous sulfate with vitamin C. Address the underlying cause.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Premenopausal women (menstrual blood loss), pregnant women, infants, adolescents, anyone with chronic GI blood loss or poor dietary intake.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Heavy menstrual bleeding, poor diet, malabsorption (celiac, IBD), chronic GI blood loss (ulcer, colorectal cancer in older patients), pregnancy.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Fatigue, pallor, dyspnea on exertion, palpitations. Signs: koilonychia (spoon nails), angular cheilitis, glossitis, pagophagia (pica for ice). Mood disturbance and cognitive fog are common and reversible.
explanation_detail.moa: Iron deficiency → impaired haemoglobin synthesis → microcytic hypochromic RBCs → reduced oxygen carrying capacity → compensatory tachycardia, high-output murmur. Ferritin depletes first (before Hgb falls).
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Microcytic hypochromic anaemia, low ferritin, low serum iron, high TIBC, koilonychia, pagophagia, pencil cells, reactive thrombocytosis.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Low Hgb, low MCV (<80), low MCH, high RDW, low ferritin (most sensitive), low serum iron, high TIBC. Peripheral smear: pencil cells, target cells, hypochromic microcytic RBCs.
explanation_detail.treatment: Oral ferrous sulfate 325mg TID with vitamin C (enhances absorption). Take on empty stomach if tolerated. Reticulocytosis in 5-10 days, Hgb normalises in 4-8 weeks. Continue 3-6 months after normalisation to replete stores. Treat underlying cause.

fennick_opening: "Her supervisor noticed before she did. Think about that."

title: The Morning Piece
polaroid_caption_success: On a Tuesday morning in May, he played the whole piece without stopping. His student said it was correct. Every note. She did not tell him that. She just listened.
polaroid_caption_failure: He played every morning until he didn't. The piece was always the same. No one ever learned its name.

chart.cc: My student brought me. I told her I was fine. I am not sure what fine means anymore.
chart.hpi: He was brought in by a former student who has visited three times a week since his wife died seven months ago. He lives alone. She finds him at the piano each time — always the same piece. On good days he is entirely himself. On bad days he asks where his wife is. He has fallen twice this month. He has been sleeping in the armchair. She found him on the floor Thursday morning. He told her he had been performing.
chart.pmh: Hypertension — well controlled. No prior neurological history. No diabetes.
chart.fh: Father: died of 'memory problems' in his eighties, details unknown.
chart.sh: Retired concert pianist. Widowed seven months ago. Lives alone. Does not drink. Does not smoke. Plays piano daily — has done so for sixty years.

cc_after_addl_hx: I don't remember choosing the piece. I just play it.
cc_after_pe: You want me to walk with my eyes closed. On a good day I could do that.
cc_after_imaging: What does the scan look for exactly.
cc_after_hint: Something is wrong with the mechanism. I know that much.
cc_after_dx: I see. Does that mean — will I still play.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Good days and bad days. Not a steady slope. That pattern is not arbitrary."
fennick_after_pe: "Upper motor. Lower motor. Both. In the same patient. The cord is not the problem."
fennick_after_imaging: "Posterior cortex. DaTscan. You have two pieces. Put them together."
additional_hx: He corrects one detail immediately: he did not fall on Thursday. He was performing. He makes this distinction seriously. He describes some days as completely clear — last Monday he caught a fingering error in a student's Scarlatti. Other days the flat feels unfamiliar. Visitors take a moment to resolve. He mentions his wife once: 'She would have wanted me to keep playing.' No sadness. A fact. He has slept in the armchair for six weeks. The bed feels dangerous, he says, and cannot explain why. He woke with a bruised wrist two weeks ago. Doesn't know how. He mentions, as an afterthought, that sometimes when he plays there is someone watching. He considers this a good sign.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "I don't remember choosing the piece. I just play it."
pe_findings: Thin, upright. Mild masked facies, reduced blink rate. Cogwheel rigidity bilateral upper limbs, asymmetric R>L. Bradykinesia on finger tapping. Gait: stooped, reduced arm swing, short steps. Pull test: mildly positive. No resting tremor. MMSE 21/30 — orientation, recall, drawing. Clock drawing: severely impaired, posterior cortical pattern. Romberg negative. No focal weakness.
imaging: MRI Brain: Cortical atrophy, posterior predominant — parietal and occipital regions disproportionately affected. Mild hippocampal atrophy, less severe than expected for degree of cognitive impairment. No acute infarct. No mass lesion. No normal pressure hydrocephalus pattern. DaTscan (Dopamine Transporter Scan): Reduced dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral putamen and caudate — asymmetric, right greater than left. Markedly abnormal pattern.

special_test.name: Polysomnography (Sleep Study)
special_test.result: REM sleep without atonia confirmed. During REM sleep: complex vocalizations, limb movements, brief episode of sitting up with arms extended — posture consistent with performing. No seizure activity. No apnoea. Findings confirm REM sleep behavior disorder.

memory_fragments.cbc: There is a photograph on top of the piano. Third row, center. He hasn't looked at it in months.
memory_fragments.datscan: He fell on Thursday. He said he was performing. Both are true.
memory_fragments.rbdsleep: There's someone in the third row sometimes. He plays more carefully when they're there.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. WBC 6.8, Hgb 13.4, Plt 221. No inflammatory process.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 1.0, glucose 96, LFTs normal, TSH 1.8. No metabolic cause for cognitive change.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No infection.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal.

hints: The cognitive fluctuation — sharp on Monday, asking where his wife is on Wednesday. The motor findings without a resting tremor. The posterior cortical pattern on imaging. These are not three separate problems.

He sees a woman in the third seat. He plays better when she is there. The student sees an empty chair. This symptom has a specific character — formed, detailed, non-frightening. That character is diagnostically meaningful.
The DaTscan shows dopaminergic deficit. The posterior cortical atrophy on MRI. The RBD. The fluctuating cognition. One diagnosis produces all four simultaneously.

Before you choose a treatment — consider what class of drug will be given to manage his behavioral symptoms on the ward. Some medications that are standard elsewhere are not standard here. The choice could kill him.

management.title: Confirm the diagnosis
management.hint: You have a clinical picture and two imaging results. What confirms and characterizes the diagnosis before committing to treatment?

options.sub: Characterize cognitive profile and confirm RBD
options.feedback_correct: Confirmed. Posterior cortical pattern on neuropsych — not hippocampal dominant. Polysomnography elevates RBD from symptom to diagnostic criterion.
options.pt_remark_correct: More tests. I don't mind tests. I mind not knowing.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "The polysomnography is not optional. Without it you have a clinical impression. With it you have a diagnosis."

options.text: Lumbar puncture for CSF tau and amyloid-beta
options.feedback_wrong: Alzheimer biomarkers may be mildly abnormal but are not diagnostic here and do not change management. The clinical picture, DaTscan, and posterior imaging point away from typical Alzheimer's. The fluctuation and motor findings demand a different framework.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A needle in the spine. If that is what it takes.

options.feedback_wrong: The fluctuating cognition and witnessed nocturnal events could superficially suggest epilepsy, but the full picture — parkinsonism, posterior atrophy, DaTscan deficit, visual phenomena — is not a seizure syndrome. EEG is not the priority.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Seizures. I hadn't thought of that.

options.feedback_wrong: Treating before confirming is imprecise medicine. The choice of cholinesterase inhibitor matters — not all are equivalent here — and the diagnosis changes what you counsel the patient and family about prognosis and drug avoidance.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A medication. Already. Before we know what this is.

management.title: First-line pharmacotherapy
management.hint: Diagnosis confirmed. Cognitive fluctuation is the primary symptom affecting his function. What do you start — and what do you absolutely avoid?

options.text: Rivastigmine — cholinesterase inhibitor, start low and titrate
options.sub: Cholinesterase inhibitor — start 1.5mg BID, titrate slowly
options.feedback_correct: Rivastigmine — strongest cholinesterase inhibitor evidence in this diagnosis. 1.5mg BID with food, titrate slowly. Set expectations: this manages symptoms. It does not alter the underlying progression.
options.pt_remark_correct: A patch. I can do a patch. Mira will make sure I use it.

reactive_stage.fennick_transition: "The rivastigmine is working. Now look at what the nights are doing to him."
reactive_stage.pt_remark_transition: I played something yesterday. I remembered the fingering for the third movement. I haven't remembered that in months.
reactive_stage.vitals_note: Night staff, 03:10: found seated beside bed, right arm extended. Vocalizing. Bruising right forearm consistent with impact. Second similar episode this week. Oriented on rousing but agitated x4 min.
reactive_stage.title: Night staff report — new development
reactive_stage.hint: The treatment is working on one axis. Something else has surfaced that you already had evidence for — but now it is causing injury. Address it directly.

options.text: Low-dose clonazepam or melatonin at night
options.feedback_correct: Correct. RBD — acting out dreams without muscle atonia. Low-dose clonazepam 0.25mg or melatonin at night. Clear the floor beside the bed.
options.pt_remark_correct: I was performing. I was in the hall. She was in the third row. I was playing the right notes.
options.fennick_correct_comment: "Clonazepam. The mechanism is GABA-A. It suppresses the brainstem circuit that is failing to hold the body still during REM. It will not slow the disease. It will stop him breaking his wrist."

options.feedback_wrong: Haloperidol blocks dopamine receptors. In this diagnosis the dopaminergic system is already compromised. The sensitivity reaction — rigidity, impaired consciousness, autonomic instability — occurs in over 50% of cases. It can be fatal.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something to make me sleep. Yes. I would like to sleep properly.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "That drug has killed patients with this diagnosis. Never. Not even once."

options.feedback_wrong: The rivastigmine is working. These are separate mechanisms — cholinergic deficit and REM motor disinhibition. You cannot dose your way out of the second one.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More of the same. Alright.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "More of the same does not fix a different problem."

options.feedback_wrong: Zolpidem sedates. It does not suppress REM motor activity. In an elderly patient with parkinsonism it increases fall risk. You are making the night more dangerous, not safer.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A sleeping pill. I've never taken those.
options.fennick_wrong_comment: "Sedation is not sleep architecture. These are not the same thing."

reactive_stage.description: Found on floor beside bed at 03:10 — arm extended, vocalizing. Second episode this week. Bruised right forearm.

options.fennick_correct_comment: "The cholinergic deficit here is more severe than in most dementias. That is why this drug works better here than anywhere else. It is also why these patients decompensate so badly if you block those receptors instead."

options.feedback_wrong: He has no intrinsic factor. Wrong disease, wrong reasoning.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Another medication. I hope it doesn't make my hands slow.

options.feedback_wrong: Levodopa can cautiously address motor symptoms but addressing cognitive decline — which is the presenting problem and what is destroying his ability to function — comes first. Levodopa also risks worsening hallucinations, which are already present.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The shaking. Yes. The shaking has been bothering me.

options.feedback_wrong: Haloperidol can precipitate a severe, potentially fatal neuroleptic sensitivity reaction in this diagnosis — acute parkinsonism, impaired consciousness, autonomic instability. Typical antipsychotics are contraindicated. This would be catastrophic.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something to calm the difficult days. Yes. Those days are very difficult.

management.title: Disposition and safeguarding
management.hint: He is medically stabilized. He lives alone. What does going home safely require?

options.text: Discharge with carer support, medication alert card, specialist follow-up, no typical antipsychotics in notes
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Stable enough to go home. Familiar environment is protective. The medication alert card follows him everywhere — no haloperidol, no typical antipsychotics.
options.pt_remark_correct: I want to go home. The piano is there. I need to finish the piece.

options.text: Admit to memory care ward indefinitely
options.feedback_wrong: He is medically stable, the acute situation is addressed, and admission to an unfamiliar environment — with a diagnosis that causes fluctuating cognition and visual phenomena — risks significant decompensation. The familiar environment of his flat is protective, not hazardous, with appropriate support.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A ward. I see. Will there be a piano.

options.text: Discharge with olanzapine PRN for behavioral episodes
options.feedback_wrong: Olanzapine carries significant neuroleptic sensitivity risk in this diagnosis. PRN antipsychotics in the community without specialist supervision is dangerous. This is not appropriate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A pill for the bad days. That sounds manageable.

options.text: Discharge without follow-up — stable on current medications
options.feedback_wrong: This is a progressive neurodegenerative condition with a specific and serious medication risk profile. Discharging without specialist follow-up, without a care plan, without a medication alert — and without telling Mira what to watch for — is abandonment dressed as efficiency.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can manage on my own. I have always managed on my own.

fennick_case_complete: "We bought him time. That is the job. Put the chart down."

report_template.presenting: progressive cognitive fluctuation, parkinsonism, nocturnal behavioral episodes, and recurrent falls — brought in by his student after witnessed floor fall

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: cognitive fluctuation with sharp good days and disoriented bad days, visual phenomena of a formed non-threatening nature, REM behavioral episodes with injury, piano playing preserved as isolated procedural memory
pertinent_positives.pe: cogwheel rigidity asymmetric right greater than left, bradykinesia, posterior cortical clock drawing impairment, MMSE 21/30, marked intratest fluctuation
pertinent_positives.labs: normal metabolic panel, TSH normal — no reversible cause
pertinent_positives.imaging: MRI posterior predominant cortical atrophy, DaTscan reduced dopamine transporter uptake bilateral putamen asymmetric
pertinent_positives.special: polysomnography confirming REM sleep without atonia with complex motor behaviors

report_template.assessment: Lewy Body Dementia — probable, meeting core and supportive criteria
report_template.plan: rivastigmine titration, clonazepam for RBD, medication alert card documenting neuroleptic sensitivity, community care package, neurology/specialist memory service follow-up, carer education

explanation: Lewy Body Dementia is characterized by four core features: cognitive fluctuation, visual hallucinations (formed, detailed, non-threatening), parkinsonism, and REM sleep behavior disorder. DaTscan shows dopaminergic deficit. MRI shows posterior cortical predominance. Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine) are first-line — they have stronger evidence here than in Alzheimer's. Neuroleptic sensitivity is the critical management risk: typical antipsychotics and many atypicals can cause severe, potentially fatal reactions. This is the fact that must follow this patient everywhere.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Adults over 60. Second most common cause of degenerative dementia after Alzheimer's. Frequently misdiagnosed as Alzheimer's or Parkinson's Disease Dementia.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Age, male sex, family history. REM sleep behavior disorder can precede dementia by decades — it is a prodromal marker.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Fluctuating cognition (not steady decline), recurrent formed visual hallucinations, parkinsonism (bradykinesia, rigidity — resting tremor less prominent than PD), REM sleep behavior disorder. Posterior cortical atrophy on MRI. DaTscan abnormal.
explanation_detail.moa: Abnormal alpha-synuclein aggregates (Lewy bodies) in cortical and subcortical neurons — cholinergic and dopaminergic systems primarily affected. Differs from PD dementia by temporal relationship: dementia precedes or occurs simultaneously with parkinsonism.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations (formed, detailed), parkinsonism, RBD, DaTscan positive, posterior cortical atrophy, neuroleptic sensitivity, rivastigmine.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: No specific blood marker. DaTscan: reduced uptake in putamen and caudate. PSG: REM without atonia. MRI: posterior > hippocampal atrophy (opposite of Alzheimer's).
explanation_detail.treatment: Rivastigmine (strongest evidence of all dementias). Clonazepam or melatonin for RBD. Levodopa cautiously for motor symptoms. ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION: typical antipsychotics, many atypicals (risperidone, olanzapine — high sensitivity risk). Quetiapine or clozapine if antipsychotic unavoidable.

fennick_opening: "He was brought in by someone else. Note that."
